title: Urban Geography
text:
Note



Core I Paper XV URBAN GEOGRAPHY
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I: Learning Outcome:
Get an idea about development of urban Geography and its relevance.
Concept of Urban, Urbanization and Urbanism; Nature and scope of urban geography; Urban geography: a sub discipline (evolution); Classification of towns and cities: Census (Census vs.  statutory towns) and functional classification (million plus cities, met ropolitan, megalopolitan, cosmopolitan, compact city, virtual city, network city, world class city, global city, inclusive city, sustainable city and concept and planning of smart city).
Unit -II: Learning Outcome:
Understand the concepts and critically recent concepts in urban geography
Origin and evolution of urban settlements; Hierarchy of urban settlements; concept of urban region; slum: a unique character of Indian cities, concept of ghettoization and gentrification; Urban public places; Peri -urban: concept, challenges and opportuniti es; unequal cities; urban planning, policy and governance.
Unit -III:
Learning Outcome:
Comprehend the emerging patterns of urban settlements and related issues
Models of Internal Structure of the City -Burgess, Hoyt, Harris and Ullman; Central Place theory of Christaller; Trends and patterns of urbanization in World and India; Urbanization: problems and opportunities; Recent urban changes.
Unit -IV: Practical
Learning Outcome:
To ensure the practical knowledge on urban geography.
1. Calculate the applicability of Law of Primate City and Rank Size Rule in the context of India.
2. Analyse the trends of urban growth in Odisha by using census data.
3. Identify the major cities of Odisha (Class I) and local urban centres of your concern district in the Odisha map.




Content for 01 Concept Classification coming soon.



Content for 02 Evolution Structure coming soon.



Content for 03 Models Trends coming soon.



Content for 04 Practical coming soon.

title: Trade and Transport
text: Welcome to the Trade and Transport module of Geography OpenCourseWare. This course examines the spatial patterns of trade and the transportation systems that connect producers to consumers across local, national, and global scales.

title: Trade and Transport
text:
UGC NET Syllabus

Theories and Models of spatial interaction (Edward Ullman and M. E. Hurst).
Measures and Indices of connectivity and accessibility.
Spatial Flow Models: Gravity Model and its variants.
World Trade Organisation, Globalisation and Liberalisation and World Trade Patterns.
Problems and Prospects of Inter and Intra Regional Cooperation and Trade.

title: Trade and Transport
text:
Models in Transportation
Models of transportation and land use help predict spatial interactions, development patterns, and the efficiency of transport networks.

Classifications of Models

1. Location and Accessibility Models

Lowny Model (1964): The first significant model of transport and land-use purposes. It was basically demand-driven and used a gravity model to allocate activities.
Central Place Model: Modifications of the original central place models often serve as the basis for modern accessibility studies.



2. Spatial Interaction / Interdependence Models

Integrated Transport Land Use Package (ITLUP): Developed by Putman, it classifies models into three elemental things:

DRAM (Disaggregated Residential Allocation Model)
EAM (Employment Allocation Model)
TDM (Travel Demand Model)

LILT: Least Integrated Landuse Transport Model (Machett).
MEPLAN: Focuses on the interaction between market sectors and the transport network.
TRANVS / PECAS / DELTA: Advanced simulation models for urban and regional growth.



3. Distribution and Flow Models

Lowny-based Gravity Models: Use gravity to allocate export industry workers to residences.
IRPUD: A model developed at Macro, Meso, and Micro scales to understand inter-regional services, new jobs, or job changes.
Cellular Automata: Used to explain complex behaviors in social and natural processes (vegetation, hydrology, soil) by considering neighborhood rules.

title: Trade and Transport
text:
Transport System of India
India possesses a vast and diverse transport infrastructure comprising roads, railways, airways, and waterways.

1. Road Network and National Highways
National Highways (NH) are the spinal cord of India’s road network. * NH1: Delhi to Amritsar (via Ambala and Jalandhar). * NH1A: Jalandhar to Uri. * NH2: Delhi to Kolkata. * NH3: Agra to Mumbai. * NH7: Varanasi to Kanyakumari (longest highway in India). * NH8: Delhi to Mumbai (via Jaipur, Ahmedabad, Vadodara). * NH9: Pune to Machilipatnam. * NH10: Delhi to Fazilka (proceeding to Indo-Pak border). * NH24: Delhi to Lucknow. * NH39: Numaligarh to Indo-Myanmar border.
Highway Numbering Convention: * Even numbers (2, 4, 6, …) usually run North-South. * Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, …) usually run East-West.


2. Expressways

Mahatma Gandhi Expressway: Ahmedabad to Vadodara.
Yamuna Expressway: Greater Noida to Agra (India’s longest 6-lane controlled-access expressway).
Mumbai-Pune Expressway: First 6-lane high-speed expressway.



3. Railways
India has the 4th largest railway network in the world. * History: First railway proposal in Madras (1832). On 16th April 1853, the first passenger train was hauled from Boribunder (Mumbai) to Thane. * Railway Zones: * Central: Mumbai (CST) * Eastern: Kolkata * Northern: New Delhi (Baroda House) * Northeast Frontier: Maligaon (Guwahati) * Western: Mumbai (Churchgate) * High-Speed Corridors: The Mumbai-Ahmedabad high-speed railway corridor (Bullet Train) was expected to begin in June 2018 with a target completion in August 2022.


4. Waterways

Inland Waterways: Significant for cargo movement, particularly in the Ganga and Brahmaputra rivers.
Ports: Crucial for international trade, with major ports on both the Eastern and Western coasts.

title: Trade and Transport
text:
International Trade
The flow of goods and services across borders.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






ImportantTrade Theory & Practice




Comparative Advantage (Ricardo): Why nations trade based on relative production efficiency.
Heckscher-Ohlin Model: Trade based on factor endowments (labor, capital, land).
Terms of Trade: Export-import price ratios and their impact on developing nations.
Trade Organizations: WTO, regional blocs (EU, ASEAN, NAFTA/USMCA, RCEP).
Free Trade vs. Protectionism: Tariffs, quotas, subsidies, and trade wars.

title: Trade and Transport
text:
Modes of Transport
Characteristics and geographic significance of different transport modes.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






TipRoad, Rail, Water & Air




Road Transport: Flexibility, last-mile connectivity, highway networks, and expressways.
Rail Transport: Bulk freight, high-speed rail, trans-continental lines, and dedicated freight corridors.
Water Transport: Inland waterways, maritime shipping, ports, and containerization.
Air Transport: Hub-and-spoke models, international air routes, and logistics hubs.
Pipelines: Crude oil, natural gas, and product pipeline networks.

title: Social & Cultural Geography
text:
Note



Core I Paper XXIII Social and Cultural Geography
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I: Learning Outcome:
Understand the spatial dimensions of socio -cultural diversities for promoting equality, social
justice and wellbeing, harmony.
Definition and Nature of Social Geography; Social Geography in the Realm of Social Sciences; Concept of Social Structure and its importance in Indian Social Realities; Understanding early Cultural differentiation and Social Formation in India: Spatial distribution of Sodasa (Sixteen) Mahajanapadas , Mughal Provinces and British Provinces.
Unit -II Learning Outcome:
enhance the socially aware conscience by knowing the social and cultural components of social groups, regions, and localities for sustainable coexistence
Concepts of Social Justice and Wellbeing; Social Inclusion and Exclusion: Conceptual understandings; Geographies of Gated Communities and Slums; Social Empowerment: Major Schemes of Social Empowerment in India; Affordable Housing Schemes for Low and Modera te-income Resident in India: Concept and Government Initiatives in India (Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana and Biju Pucca Ghar Yojana).
Unit -III:
Learning Outcome:
Comprehend the cultural dimensions of space and their correlates.
Meaning and definition of Culture and Cultural traits, Types of Culture: Folk and Popular Culture, Cultural Realms of the World, Cultural Hearth; Diffusion of Innovations: Spatial Dimensions; Concept, Types an d Conservation of Geo -heritage.
Unit -IV: Practical
Learning Outcome:
Highlight and ponder upon the socio -culturally induced local problems and their solutions.
1. Delineate the prominent residential localities and social morphology of your village or town/city in accordance with the distribution of housing units in terms of caste, tribe, class, religion, and ethnicity.
2. Prepare a sketch map based on field observation.
3. Identify a few Geo -heritage sites and explore the role of culture and local initiatives in its conservation.
4. Produce a brief report with field photographs.
5. Prepare a brief report on how the adoption of popular culture in the wake of cultural globalization is a major cause of environmental pollution and degradation in your localities with evidence and examples.
6. Report and Viva -Voce

title: Social & Cultural Geography
text:
Cultural Landscapes
How culture imprints itself on the visible landscape.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






TipReading Landscapes




Carl Sauer’s Cultural Landscape: Culture as an agent shaping the natural landscape.
Vernacular Architecture: Building styles reflecting climate, materials, and cultural traditions.
Agricultural Landscapes: Terraced fields, paddy landscapes, pastoral scenes, and plantation estates.
Urban Cultural Landscapes: Markets, heritage zones, religious quarters, and cosmopolitan districts.
UNESCO World Heritage Sites: Cultural and mixed-heritage sites as expressions of geographic identity.

title: Social & Cultural Geography
text:
Culture & Geography
The spatial expression of cultural systems.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






NoteCultural Foundations




Definition of Culture: Learned behavior, shared values, beliefs, customs, and material artifacts.
Cultural Diffusion: Carl Sauer and the spread of cultural traits — expansion, relocation, hierarchical, and contagious diffusion.
Cultural Regions: Formal, functional, and vernacular cultural regions.
Cultural Ecology: Human adaptation to environmental conditions — Julian Steward’s framework.
Acculturation & Assimilation: Cultural mixing, blending, and the preservation of identity.




Demographic attributes: sex-ratio, age structure, literacy rate, work-force, dependency ratio, longevity

How global connectivity reshapes culture and place.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

Cultural Homogenization: McDonaldization, Americanization, and global consumer culture.
Glocalization: The adaptation of global products and ideas to local contexts.

Diaspora & Transnationalism: Maintaining cultural identity across borders — Indian, Chinese, and African diasporas.
Cultural Preservation: Museums, intangible heritage (UNESCO), and grassroots cultural movements.

title: Research Methodology
text:
Note



Core I Paper XVI Research Methodology
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I Learning Outcome:
The learners will get the initial training on various steps involved in geographical research.
Research in Geography: Meaning and significance; Need for research; Types of research; Research Design; Defining research problems; Development of theoretical background - literature review, identifying research gap and research questions and objectives of study; Hypothesis building; Framewo rk of research writing; research ethics.
Unit -II Learning Outcome:
They will develop the idea on fundamentals of research methodology including data collection, methodology and report writing.
Definition and type of research materials and methods; Methods of data collection - primary and secondary; Differentiating Questionnaire and Schedule; Techniques of writing dissertation: preparing drafts, abstract and keywords, citations, references, biblio graphy; Plagiarism: classification and prevention.
Unit -III Learning Outcome:
This course also aims to develop fundamental research aptitude among all the students. Besides the course explores the theoretical background to some extent
Fieldwork in Geographical studies: role and significance; Selection of study area and objectives; Pre -field academic preparations. Ethics of fieldwork. Field techniques and tools: Observation (participant, non -participant), questionnaires (open, LOsed, str uctured, non - structured); Interview; Landscape survey using transects and quadrants, constructing a sketch, photo and video recording; Post -field tabulation, processing and analysis of quantitative and qualitative data; Fieldwork: logistics and handling of emergencies.
Unit IV: Practical
Learning Outcome:
Above all consists of practical components with programs built on open source. Basic programming is included
1. Identification of research problem
2. Keyword and search query development
3. Searching literature using google scholar and dimension
4. Effective summarizing of literature and writing literature review
5. Problematization/mapping research gap
6. Framing research objective and methodology
7. Development and submission of a research proposal
8. Viva -Voce




Content for 01 Meaning Design coming soon.



Content for 02 Materials Methods coming soon.



Content for 03 Fieldwork coming soon.



Content for 04 Practical coming soon.

title: Reading Materials & Methodology
text:
Welcome to the central resource hub of Geography OpenCourseWare. Here, we outline our teaching philosophy and provide direct access to high-quality textbooks, research papers, and interactive learning modules.


Core Pillars of Our Instruction
Our teaching methodology is designed to translate complex theoretical concepts into practical, spatial insights through three core pillars:


 Self-Paced Learning
Flexible access to curated digital content, high-quality video modules, and structured reading materials, allowing students to master geography at their own speed.
 Visit YouTube Channel


 Classroom Discussion
Interactive engagement where theoretical complexities are simplified, and student doubts are addressed through expert-led dialogue and real-time feedback.


 Peer Interaction
A collaborative environment that encourages peer-to-peer knowledge sharing, group analysis of spatial data, and diverse geographical perspectives.





Efficiency of Learning
We base our methodology on Academic Research (Chi et al., 1989) regarding self-explanations and the “Learning Pyramid.” The effectiveness of different learning modes is reflected in our design:

10% from Reading (Passive)
20% from Seeing (Visual)
30% from Hearing (Auditory)
50% from Seeing & Hearing (Multi-sensory)
70% from Collaboration (Active Learning)
80% from Doing (Application & Practical)

Our platform prioritizes the 70-80% retention bracket by encouraging student interaction and “learning by doing” through module-based exercises.



Curated E-learning Portals
To support your self-study, we recommend the following authoritative platforms:


 #### e-Adhyayan (e-Books) High-quality e-Books for postgraduate Geography courses provided by INFLIBNET.


 #### e-PG Pathshala Comprehensive curriculum-based interactive content and video lectures.


 #### NPTEL Courses Online courses in Geography and related disciplines provided by India’s top technical institutes.





Standard Reference Books
For in-depth study, we recommend the following authoritative textbooks across various sub-disciplines of Geography.

Our Pedagogical Approach

Blended Learning: A combination of high-quality video modules, interactive reading materials, and hands-on spatial exercises.
Micro-Learning Units: Complex topics are broken down into digestible fragments, ensuring better retention and easier mastery of specific curriculum points.
Syllabus Alignment: Every unit is precisely mapped to national academic standards (such as UGC NET) to ensure direct utility for exam preparation.
Visual Excellence: Geography is inherently visual. Our materials feature premium visualizations, technical diagrams, and high-resolution maps.




Student Outcomes

Critical Analysis: Ability to evaluate environmental and human interactions critically.
Technical Proficiency: Mastery of modern geographical techniques and statistical tools.
Research Readiness: Foundational knowledge required to conduct independent, high-quality geographical research.


“The study of geography is more than just memorizing places on a map. It’s about understanding the complexity of our world…”
— Barack Obama

title: Political Geography
text:
Note



Core I Paper XXII Political Geography a nd Globalisation
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I
Learning Outcome:
understand the complex contemporary world power structure and the role of geography in global politics.
Political geography: concept, nature, scope and evolution; Concept of state, nation and nation - state; Attributes of state: frontiers, boundaries, buffer zone, population, territory, sovereignty; Geopolitics: concept, and theories (Heartland and Rimland)
Unit -II
Learning Outcome:
differentiate the State, Nation and Nation State and carry research on strategies compatible to the present technological development
Political geography and systems of government: Federalism, Local self -government administration, Creation of new states; Political geography of resource conflicts: inter -state river water sharing disputes; Conflicts on forest rights and mineral resources; Politics of Displacement: Issues of relief, compensation and rehabilitation with special reference to Dams and Special Economic Zones (SEZs)
Unit -III Learning Outcome:
establish a clear idea about the relationship of India with its neighbouring countries and also about some international organizations along with their role and importance.
Geo-Economic and Political Blocks: UNO, SAARC and ASEAN, NATO, EU, OPEC and BRICS; India as a global power with special reference to G20; India and its relationship with neighbouring countries: Pakistan, China, Nepal, Bhutan, Bangladesh, Myanmar and Sri Lanka
Unit -IV: Practical
Learning Outcome:
impart knowledge about political processes operating in society, and how the global and local orders have been reshaped by the power structure and geopolitical factors.
1: Prepare a map of the performances of political parties in the last general election in the constituencies of Odisha by using digital cartography by using the data from Election Commission of India.
2: Prepare a suitable visualization on State wise percentage share of electors in India by using the data from Election Commission of India.
3: Map the types of parliamentary constituencies (SC, ST, GEN) in Odisha by using the data from Election Commission of India.
4: Mapping the Indian Ocean region: trade and maritime boundaries.

title: Political Geography
text:
UGC NET Syllabus

Heartland and Rimland Theories.
Trends and Developments in Political Geography.
Geography of Federalism, Electoral Reforms in India, Determinants of Electoral Behaviour.

Regional Organisations of Cooperation (SAARC, ASEAN, OPEC, EU), Neopolitics of World Natural Resources.

title: Political Geography
section: Definition and Scope of Political Geography
text:
Definition and Scope of Political Geography
Political Geography is the study of the spatial distribution of political processes and the ways in which these processes are affected by the physical environment.

Key Concepts

Territoriality: This concept owes much of its origin to Robert Ardrey’s book “The Territorial Imperative” (1966). He defined it as an area of space (water, earth, or air) which an animal or group of animals defends as an exclusive preserve.
Geopolitik: A term borrowed by Karl Haushofer from Rudolf Kjellén’s book “Der Staat als Lebensform”. Haushofer defined it as “a science which deals with the dependence of political events upon the soil.”
Friedrich Ratzel: Often considered the father of modern political geography, he viewed the state as a living organism (Lebensraum).

title: Political Geography
text:
Geopolitics and Global Strategic Views

1. Karl Haushofer and Geopolitik

Borrowed the term from Rudolf Kjellén.
In 1924, Haushofer began editing the journal “Zeitschrift fur Geopolitik”.
Established “The Institut” in Munich, which focused on the dependence of political events upon the “soil” (earth).



2. Saul Cohen: World Divided (1973)

In his book “Geography and Politics in a World Divided”, Cohen offered a hierarchical and regional world model.
He identified two types of regions:

Geostrategic Regions: Large-scale global divisions.
Geopolitical Regions: Subdivisions within geostrategic regions.

Cohen’s model allowed for more traditional geographical concepts to be integrated into modern global strategic thinking.

title: Political Geography
text:
Heartland Theory (Sir Halford Mackinder)
Sir Halford Mackinder, a British geographer and member of the Liberal Party, proposed the Heartland Theory, which emphasized the strategic importance of the interior of the Eurasian continent.

Historical Context & Influences

Alfred Thayer Mahan (1840-1914): Influence of sea power concept. Book: “The Influence of Sea Power upon History”.
Rudolf Kjellén: Concept of “geopolitical instinct”.
Mahan’s “Problems of Asia” (1910): Provided a strategic model for sea power.



Evolution of the Theory

1. 1904: “The Geographical Pivot of History”

Presented at the Royal Geographical Society.
Identified the Pivot Area (Eurasia) as the center of global power, protected by distance from sea power.
Linked historical generalizations with geographical formations of four regions:

Buddhism: Towards the Pacific.
Brahmanism: Towards the Indian Ocean.
Mahometanism: Towards the Indian Ocean and the Mediterranean.
Christianity: In Europe, watered by Atlantic rains.




2. 1919: “Democratic Ideals and Reality”

Replaced the term “Pivot Area” with The Heartland.
Included the Baltic Sea, Lower Danube, Black Sea, Asia Minor, Armenia, Persia, Tibet, and Mongolia.
The Dictum: > “Who rules East Europe commands the Heartland;
> Who rules the Heartland commands the World-Island;
> Who rules the World-Island commands the World.”
World-Island: Europe, Asia, and Africa.



3. 1943: “The Round World and the Winning of the Peace”

In response to WWII and USA’s involvement.
Introduced the concept of the Midland Ocean (North Atlantic) and Lenaland (the rugged, forested part of the Soviet Union east of the Yenisei River).
Mainland: Consisting of the North Atlantic Ocean, Eastern USA, and Western Europe.




Criticism

Argued that the theory was outdated by the policy of nuclear deterrence.

title: Political Geography
text:
Rimland Theory (Nicholas Spykman)
Nicholas Spykman proposed the Rimland Theory in 1944 in his book “Geography of the Peace”, as a counter to Mackinder’s Heartland-centric view.

Key Assertions

Sea Power over Land Power: Spykman emphasized the importance of ocean navigation and the discovery of sea routes to India and America as fundamental facts of world politics.
Defining the Rimland: He renamed Mackinder’s “Inner Crescent” as the Rimland. This amphibious buffer zone between the Heartland and the sea is the real key to world power.
Geography of the Rimland: It consists of the European coast land, the Arabian Middle-Eastern desert, and the Asiatic monsoon land.



Strategic Dictum
Spykman reversed Mackinder’s dictum: > “Who controls the Rimland rules Eurasia;
> Who rules Eurasia controls the destinies of the world.”


Relationship with Heartland

The Rimland has a central position with interior lines of communication.
The development of land transportation allowed the Heartland to begin competing with sea communication, but the Rimland remains the vital zone of contact and conflict.



Related Work

Donald W. Meining (1958): Heartland and Rimland in Eurasian History.

title: Political Geography
text:
Concept of Nation and State

1. State

A Political entity with a fixed territory.
Derived from the Latin word “status”.
Defined by its political sovereignty. Famous quote: “I am the state” (Louis XIV).



2. Nation

A Socio-cultural entity.
Derived from the Latin word “nasci” meaning “to be born”.
Implies common ancestry and a shared “place of origin” (homeland).
Richard Hartshorne (1950): A nation represents a feeling of kinship and belonging together — an extension of the concept of family (in-groups vs. outsiders).
John Stuart Mill: A nation is a portion of mankind united among themselves by common sympathies which do not exist between them and any others.



3. Nationalism

The feeling of belonging to a nation.
The desire of cultural-linguistic and religious groups to achieve political status that would grant them measures of self-government.

title: Political Geography
text:
Boundaries and Frontiers
In political geography, there is a distinct difference between “Boundaries” and “Frontiers.”

1. Frontiers (Area)

Nature: Geographical and zonal in nature.
Role: An integrating factor between states on either side.
Types:

Natural/Physical: Mountains, rivers, forests.
Ethnic/Anthropological: Based on human demographics.
Geometrical/Astronomical: Based on lat/long.
Political: Zones of political influence.




2. Boundaries (Line)

Nature: Political and linear in nature.
Role: A separating factor that defines the limit of sovereignty.
Evolution Process:

Arbitrary Process: Drawn without regard to physical or cultural features.
Evolutionary Process: Developing over time through settlement and use.
Arbitration Process: Settled by legal or diplomatic third parties.




Comparison



Feature
Frontier
Boundary




Form
Area/Zone
Line


Integrity
Integrating factor
Separating factor


Orientation
Outward-looking
Inward-looking


Stability
Fluctuating
Fixed by law

title: Political Geography
text:
Geography and Federalism
Federalism is a system of government where power is divided between a central authority and constituent political units.

Key Geopolitical Insights

Neumann (1955): Defined Federalism as a device to “curb the evil use of power by dividing it among a number of competing power units.”
Geographical Basis: Federalism often arises in states with large territories, diverse populations, or distinct regional identities that require a balance between unity and local autonomy.
Case Example: India’s federal structure is deeply rooted in its racial, linguistic, and ethnic diversities.

title: Political Geography
text:
Boundaries & Frontiers
Lines and zones that define political space.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






ImportantBorder Dynamics




Types of Boundaries: Natural (rivers, mountains), geometric (latitude/longitude lines), and ethnographic.
Boundary Evolution: Antecedent, subsequent, superimposed, and relict boundaries.
Boundary Disputes: Territorial, positional, resource, and functional disputes.
Frontiers vs. Boundaries: Zones of transition vs. lines of demarcation.
India’s Borders: McMahon Line, Radcliffe Line, Line of Control, and Durand Line.

title: Political Geography
text:

21st century power dynamics and spatial conflicts.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






ImportantModern Global Challenges




Territorial Conflicts: South China Sea, Kashmir, Crimea, and the Arctic.
Resource Geopolitics: Oil, water, rare earth minerals, and energy corridors.
Supranational Organizations: UN, NATO, EU, BRICS, SCO — and their geopolitical roles.
Cyberspace & Geopolitics: Digital sovereignty, information warfare, and cyber boundaries.
Climate Geopolitics: Resource scarcity, climate migration, and environmental security.





Books and Further Readings
Political Geography provides the framework for understanding spatial power dynamics. Continue with Economic Geography and Indian SocioEco for related perspectives.

Mackinder’s Heartland Theory: Whoever controls East Europe commands the Heartland, and thereby the World Island.
Spykman’s Rimland Theory: The coastal periphery as the key to global dominance.

title: Oceanography
text:
Ocean Currents
Horizontal and vertical movements of ocean water.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






ImportantGlobal Current Systems




Surface Currents: Wind-driven gyres — North Atlantic, South Atlantic, Pacific, and Indian Ocean systems.
Warm Currents: Gulf Stream, Kuroshio, Brazil Current — moderating high-latitude climates.
Cold Currents: Labrador, Canary, Benguela, Humboldt — creating coastal aridity and upwelling.
Upwelling & Downwelling: Nutrient-rich cold water rising to the surface; implications for fisheries.
El Niño & La Niña: Disruption of normal Pacific current patterns with global climate impacts.

Marine Biodiversity: Coral reefs, mangroves, and deep-sea ecosystems.

title: Oceanography
text:

The ocean’s critical role in the global climate system.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






ImportantClimate-Ocean Coupling




Heat Transport: Oceans as the planet’s primary heat reservoirs and redistributors.

Sea Level Rise: Thermal expansion and ice sheet melt — projected impacts.
Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD): Regional climate oscillation affecting the Indian subcontinent.





Books and Further Readings
Oceanography deepens your understanding of Earth’s hydrosphere. Explore Climatology and Environmental for complementary perspectives on Earth systems.

title: Oceanography
text:
Ocean Floor Topography
The underwater landscape of the Earth.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






NoteSubmarine Relief Features




Continental Shelf: Shallow, gently sloping extension of continents — rich in marine resources.
Continental Slope: Steep transition zone between the shelf and deep ocean floor.
Abyssal Plains: Flat, deep-ocean floor covered in fine sediments.
Mid-Ocean Ridges: The longest mountain chains on Earth — sites of seafloor spreading.
Ocean Trenches: The deepest zones — subduction boundaries (Mariana Trench, 11,034 m).
Seamounts & Guyots: Volcanic peaks rising from the ocean floor.

title: Oceanography
text:

Physical properties governing ocean behavior.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






TipOcean Water Properties




Temperature Distribution: Horizontal (latitude-based) and vertical (thermocline) patterns.
Salinity: Average 35 ppt — influenced by evaporation, precipitation, river input, and ice formation.
Thermohaline Circulation: Density-driven deep ocean circulation connecting the global ocean system.
Water Masses: Identification and tracking of distinct water bodies by their T-S characteristics.

text:

Oscillations and rhythmic movements of ocean water.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






NoteWave & Tidal Dynamics




Wave Generation: Wind speed, duration, and fetch controlling wave characteristics.
Wave Erosion & Deposition: Coastal landform creation through wave action.
Tides: Gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun — spring and neap tides.
Tidal Patterns: Diurnal, semi-diurnal, and mixed tidal regimes.
Tidal Energy: Harnessing tidal range and tidal currents for power generation.

title: Natural Hazards and Disaster Management
text:
Note



Core I Paper XVII Natural Hazards a nd Disaster Management
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I: Learning Outcome: To understand the nature and scope of Climate Change and Disaster Management.
Understanding Climate Change: Overview of climate change:Definition of climate change and climate variability, climate risks, Disasters, Hazards, Risk, Vulnerability - Definition, Concept and Classification. Global warming, Greenhouse effect, sea level rise , Climate change adaptation strategy.
Unit -II: Learning Outcome:
To learn the Climate Change and Disaster Adaptation and Mitigation strategy.
Physical, social and economic vulnerability, disaster management - Disaster Management Cycle, Community Based Disaster Management. Role of NDMA and SDMA in Disaster Mitigation Strategy.
Unit -III:
Learning Outcome:
To learn the extreme climatic events and their effects.
Types of Disasters: Natural and Man made, Floods, Droughts, Cyclones, thunderstorms, tornado, Earthquakes and landslides, lightning, Oil spills, Chemical and Biological attacks.
Unit -IV: Practical:
Learning Outcome:
To apply the tools and techniques for disaster awareness and mitigation.
Project report/Term paper
1. Disaster management: International - Yokohama Strategy for a Safer World 1994,
2. Hyogo framework for Action 2005 -2015; Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015 -2030;
3. Indian Policy for disaster management: Disaster Management Act 2005,
4. 10 point Agenda of Prime minister on DRR.
5. Viva -Voce.
Suggestive readings:
Text Books : ✓ Ravindranath, N.H., & Joshi, P.K. (2012). Climate change, disasters and vulnerabilities in India. Springer.
✓ Singh, S. (2021). Disaster Management. Pravalika Publication.

✓ Bryant, E. (2004). Natural Hazards. Cambridge University Press, India
✓ Barry, R.G, Chorley R.J. 2009. Atmosphere Weather and Climate., Routledge.
✓ Huq, S., Rajan, S.C., & Pachauri, R.K. (2014). Climate change and disaster management in India. Springer.

✓ Smith, Keith (2013). Environmental Hazards: Assessing risk and reducing disasters. Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group.

title: Natural Hazards and Disaster Management
text:
Note



Core I Paper XVII Natural Hazards a nd Disaster Management
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I: Learning Outcome: To understand the nature and scope of Climate Change and Disaster Management.
Understanding Climate Change: Overview of climate change:Definition of climate change and climate variability, climate risks, Disasters, Hazards, Risk, Vulnerability - Definition, Concept and Classification. Global warming, Greenhouse effect, sea level rise , Climate change adaptation strategy.
Unit -II: Learning Outcome:
To learn the Climate Change and Disaster Adaptation and Mitigation strategy.
Physical, social and economic vulnerability, disaster management - Disaster Management Cycle, Community Based Disaster Management. Role of NDMA and SDMA in Disaster Mitigation Strategy.
Unit -III:
Learning Outcome:
To learn the extreme climatic events and their effects.
Types of Disasters: Natural and Man made, Floods, Droughts, Cyclones, thunderstorms, tornado, Earthquakes and landslides, lightning, Oil spills, Chemical and Biological attacks.
Unit -IV: Practical:
Learning Outcome:
To apply the tools and techniques for disaster awareness and mitigation.
Project report/Term paper
1. Disaster management: International - Yokohama Strategy for a Safer World 1994,
2. Hyogo framework for Action 2005 -2015; Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015 -2030;
3. Indian Policy for disaster management: Disaster Management Act 2005,
4. 10 point Agenda of Prime minister on DRR.
5. Viva -Voce.

title: Indian Socio-Economic Geography
text: Welcome to the Indian Socio-Economic Geography module of Geography OpenCourseWare. This course provides a comprehensive understanding of the socio-economic landscape of India — including population dynamics, urbanization patterns, industrial development, agricultural change, and regional disparities.

title: Indian Socio-Economic Geography
text:
UGC NET Syllabus

Population Characteristics (spatial patterns of distribution), Growth and Composition, Determinants of Population, Population Policies in India.
Agriculture (Production, Productivity and Yield of Major Food Crops), Major Crop Regions, Regional Variations in Agricultural Development, Environmental, Technological and Institutional Factors affecting Indian Agriculture; Agro-Climatic Zones, Green Revolution, Food Security and Right to Food.
Industrial Development since Independence, Industrial Regions and their characteristics, Industrial Policies in India.
Development and Patterns of Transport Networks (railways, roadways, waterways, airways and pipelines), Internal and External Trade, Regional Development Planning in India.
Globalisation and its impact on Indian Economy.

title: Indian Socio-Economic Geography
text:
India’s Population Dynamics
Demographic characteristics and trends.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






NoteDemographic Profile




Population Size & Growth: From 361 million (1951) to 1.4 billion — trends, projections, and demographic dividend.
Density & Distribution: Bihar, West Bengal, and Kerala vs. Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram.
Sex Ratio: Child sex ratio concerns — Haryana, Punjab, Rajasthan vs. Kerala, Meghalaya.
Literacy: Interstate variations — Kerala (96%) vs. Bihar (62%) — gender and rural-urban gaps.
Religious & Linguistic Diversity: Scheduled languages, religious distribution, and the linguistic map of India.
Tribal Population: Scheduled Tribes — distribution, development, and rights (PESA, FRA).

title: Indian Socio-Economic Geography
text:
Indian Agriculture
The backbone of India’s economy.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






ImportantAgricultural Landscape




Crop Seasons: Kharif (rice, jowar, bajra), Rabi (wheat, mustard, gram), and Zaid (watermelon, cucumber).
Irrigation: Canals, tanks, wells, tubewells, and the irrigation revolution — Green and White Revolutions.
Agricultural Problems: Fragmentation, lack of credit, price volatility, and farmer suicides.
Agrarian Reforms: Land reforms, MSP, PM-KISAN, Kisan Credit Card, and cooperative movement.
Agro-Climatic Zones: NARP (15 zones) — scientific regional planning for agricultural development.
Allied Activities: Animal husbandry, fisheries, sericulture, and horticulture.

title: Indian Socio-Economic Geography
text:
Industrial Development
India’s industrial geography and policy.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






NoteIndustrial Landscape




Major Industries: Iron & steel, textiles, petrochemicals, IT, pharmaceuticals, and automobiles.
Industrial Regions: Mumbai-Pune, Bangalore, Hooghly, Ahmedabad-Vadodara, and Chotanagpur.
Industrial Policy: From License Raj to LPG reforms (1991) — privatization, globalization, and FDI.
Special Economic Zones (SEZs): Policy, performance, and spatial distribution.
Make in India & Atmanirbhar Bharat: Contemporary industrial promotion strategies.
Cottage & Small-Scale Industries: MSME sector — khadi, handicrafts, and decentralized production.




Issues related to environmental pollution

Content for Linkage of rivers coming soon.


Population explosion and food security
Content for Population explosion and food security coming soon.


Principles of environmental impact assessment and environmental management
Content for Principles of environmental impact assessment and environmental management coming soon.


Problems of agrarian and industrial unrest
Content for Problems of agrarian and industrial unrest coming soon.


Regional disparities in economic development

title: Indian Socio-Economic Geography
text:
Regional Disparities & Planning
Addressing India’s development imbalance.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






ImportantDevelopment Planning




Inter-State Disparities: Per capita income variation — Goa, Kerala, Tamil Nadu vs. Bihar, UP, Jharkhand.
HDI Ranking: State-wise Human Development Index — education, health, and income dimensions.
Backward Region Development: BRGF, Aspirational Districts, and hill area development programmes.
Border Area Development: BADP for India’s peripheral and strategically important border regions.
North-East India: Special economic and development challenges — Look East / Act East Policy.
NITI Aayog SDG Index: Tracking India’s progress on Sustainable Development Goals by state.





Books and Further Readings
Indian Socio-Economic Geography provides the lens for understanding India’s development challenges and opportunities. Continue with Indian Physical and Regional Development for comprehensive India coverage.

title: Indian Socio-Economic Geography
text:
Transport & Communication
India’s connectivity infrastructure.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






TipNational Connectivity




Indian Railways: Zones, gauge conversion, dedicated freight corridors, and high-speed rail.
National Highways: Golden Quadrilateral, NHDP, Bharatmala, and expressway development.
Ports & Shipping: 12 major ports, Sagarmala Project, and coastal shipping.
Civil Aviation: Airport modernization, UDAN regional connectivity, and air traffic growth.
Telecommunications: Digital India, BharatNet, and 5G rollout — bridging the digital divide.

title: Indian Socio-Economic Geography
text:
Urbanization in India
Urban growth, challenges, and planning.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






TipUrban India




Census Definition: Statutory (municipal bodies) and census towns (population/density/non-agricultural criteria).
Urban Growth Trends: Million-plus cities — from 5 (1951) to 53+ (2021); megacities (Delhi, Mumbai).
Class I-VI Towns: Census hierarchical classification of urban centers.
Urban Problems: Housing shortage, slums, water supply, sanitation, traffic congestion, and air pollution.
Urban Planning: Smart Cities Mission, AMRUT, PMAY, and HRIDAY.
Urban Governance: Municipal corporations, municipal councils, and the 74th Constitutional Amendment.

title: Human Development and Sustainable Development
text:
Note



Core I Paper XIX Human Development a nd Sustainable Development
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I Learning Outcome:
Understand the concepts of sustainable development, human health and well -being.
Development: Concept and Indicators, Physical Quality of Life Index (PQLI) and Human Development Index (HDI), Goals of Development -Economic Growth, Distributive Justice, Ecological Sustainability; Sustainable development -Concept and Approaches.
Unit -II Learning Outcome:
Comprehend the need, importance and features of SDGs.
Goals to Sustainability - UN’s 17 SDGs; Localizing sustainable development goals; Population dynamics and Sustainable Development goals, Global health and Sustainable development goals.
Unit -III Learning Outcome:
Deliberate the basic concepts of epidemiology and public health.
Concepts of Health and Wellbeing; Factors affecting Human Health and Diseases; Concepts of morbidity and its measurements, WHO International Classification of Diseases; communicable and non -communicable diseases; Epidemiological Transition Theory; Evolutio n of population and health policies in India; Health Inequalities in India with special reference to Odisha - Regional, Social and economic; India’s performance in health related SDGs.
Unit -IV: Practical
Learning Outcome:
Can accomplish various practical outlines about the SDGs.
1. Identification and estimation of development indicators
2. Measurement of PQLI
3. Measurement of HDI
4. Tracking SDGs with reference to health indicators.
5. Viva -Voce
Suggested Readings
Text Books : ✓ Sundaram, K.V. (1983). Geography of Underdevelopment: Spatial Dimensions of Underdevelopment, Concept, New Delhi
✓ United Nations (1990). Human Development Report -1990
Reference Books:
✓ Desai, V and Potter. R (2001). The Companion to Development Studies, Hodder Arnold.
✓ Gatrell, A.C. (2002). Geographies of Health: An Introduction, Blackwell,
✓ Gatrell, A., and Loytonen (1998). GIS and Health. London: Taylor and Francis Ltd.
✓ Gesler, W.M. (1992). Therapeutic landscapes: Medical issues in light of the new cultural geography. Social Science and Medicine 34: 735 –46. ✓ Meade. M.S and Emch M (2010). Medical Geography. London: The Guilford Press.
✓ Rais, A. and Learmonth, A.T.A (2018). Geographical Aspects of Health and Diseases in India. Concept Publishing Company Pvt. Ltd
✓ Smyth, Fiona (2008). Medical geography: Understanding health inequalities. Progress in Human Geography 32 (1): 119 -127. ✓ Niti Ayog (2019). Localising SDGS: Early Lessons from India,
✓ https://www.niti.gov.in/sites/default/files/2020 -07/LSDGs_July_8_Web.pdf
✓ https://sdgs.un.org/goals
✓ https://sdghelpdesk.unescap.org/e -library/localizing -sdgs-strategies -and-plans

title: Geography in News
text:
Welcome to the Geography in News section. This page serves as a dynamic hub for tracking real-world applications of geographical theories and staying updated on critical global events that shape our spatial reality.


Recent Geographical Events









Event Date
Topic
Category
Significance




April 2026
Himalayan Glacial Retreat Accelerates
Climatology
Impacts water security for 1.3 billion people.


March 2026
New Urban Heat Island Data released for Delhi
Environmental
Highlights urgent need for sustainable urban planning.


February 2026
Major Tectonic Shift detected in East African Rift
Geomorphology
Ongoing evolution of a new ocean basin.


January 2026
Remote Sensing confirms 15% recovery in Mangrove cover
GIS & RS
Success of community-led restoration projects in Sundarbans.






Critical Analysis of News Items

1. The Geopolitics of Chip Manufacturing
As global supply chains shift, Southeast Asia is emerging as a critical geographical node for semiconductor fabrication. This reflects concepts of Industrial Location and Global Trade Patterns.


2. Climate Change and Migration
Recent droughts in the Sahel region have triggered new patterns of international migration. This intersection of Environmental Geography and Population Dynamics illustrates the “push” and “pull” factors discussed in demographic models.


3. Precision Agriculture in Small Landholdings
The adoption of low-cost drones in Indian agriculture is revolutionizing the Green Revolution 2.0, emphasizing productivity without ecological depletion.




Stay Connected
We update this section monthly with insights mapped directly to your course modules. Use these real-world examples in your UPSC/UGC NET answers for extra marks!

[!TIP] Suggested Reading: Follow portals like Down To Earth, NASA Earth Observatory, and The Guardian Environment to stay ahead.

title: Field Survey: Tools and Technique
text:
Note



Core I Paper XXI Field Survey: Tools a nd Technique
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I Learning Outcome:
understand the rationale, principles and classifications of field survey
Field survey: Understand the rationales of field survey in Geography; approaches to field survey; basic principles of field survey; Classification of field survey;
Unit -II:
Learning Outcome:
understand different types of physical survey, their scope and limitations
Physical survey: Plain Table survey: meaning and definition, instruments, steps, methods, advantages & limitations; Dumpy level survey: Definition, components, steps, methods, advantages & limitations; Prismatic Compass survey: Definition, components, steps, methods, advan tages & limitations;
Unit -III: Learning Outcome:
understand the concepts of different types of quantitative and qualitative socio -economic survey
Socio -economic survey : Meaning and concept, Objectives, Sources of data, methods of data collection (quantitative and qualitative), Quantitative survey: basic concepts of sampling, planning a primary socio -economic survey, questionnaire preparation and pretesting of questionn aire, data collection using pen and paper or digital devices, types of possible errors (sampling and non -sampling), data management and quantitative data analysis; Qualitative survey; , key informant interview (KII), Focus Group discus sion (FGD), Participatory Rapid Appraisal (PRA), preparation of discussion guideline for KII and FGDs; qualitative quantitative data analysis, content analysis, ethics of primary data collection,
Unit - IV: Practical
LO: acquire skill set to conduct different physical and socio -economic primary survey in geographical research
1. Plain Table Survey/ Dumpy Level survey/ Prismatic Compass Survey
2. For a project report using socio -economic survey data in a selected village, preparation of questionnaire for a quantitative household survey and preparation of guideline for FGDs with a group of women/ men on a social issue and PRA
3. Preparation of p roject report on socio-economic survey in a village using above quantitative and qualitative data  4. Viva -Voce

title: Economic Geography
text:
Note



Core I Paper VII Economic Geography
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I:
Learning Outcome:
Understand and differentiate different types of economic activities and their utilities. Analyse the factors responsible for the location and distribution of economic activities.
Meaning and scope of economic geography; classification of economic activities; Factors affecting location of Economic Activity with special reference to agriculture, manufacturing and Services; Classification of world agricultural system of Whittlesey. Vo n Thunen theory of location of agricultural activity.
Unit –II
Learning Outcome:
Evaluate the significance and relevance of theories in relation to the location of different economic activities.
Secondary Activities: Manufacturing (Cotton Textile, Iron and Steel, Petrochemical), Major Industrial Regions of the world, Special Economic Zones and Technology Parks. Tertiary Activities: Transport (Land, Air, Water and Pipelines), Trade (National and International) ; Weber and Smith’s Industrial location Theory.
Unit -III: Learning Outcome:
Define and differentiate types of resources, their management and sustainable use.
Resource ‐ Concept and Classification, Functional Theory of Resources, Distribution, Utilization, Problems and Management of World’s Resources (Land, Water, Forest and Energy (Coal, petroleum and non -conventional), Mineral resources ( Iron, Bauxite). Resource Development Regions of India; Resource depletion, conservation and Sustainable use of resources.
Unit -IV: Practical
Learning Outcome:
Create different types of thematic maps, charts and diagrams.
1. Determination and representation of agricultural efficiency (Kendall and Bhatia method).
2. Method of delineation of crop combination regions by Weaver and Doi’s method.
3. Traffic flow diagram and Drawing of Isochrones, Isotims, Isodapanes.
4. Practical Record and viva –voce.


Welcome to the Economic Geography module of Geography OpenCourseWare. This course explores how economic activities — primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary — are distributed across space and how location, resources, and human decisions shape economic landscapes.

title: Economic Geography
text:
Note



Core I Paper VII Economic Geography
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I:
Learning Outcome:
Understand and differentiate different types of economic activities and their utilities. Analyse the factors responsible for the location and distribution of economic activities.
Meaning and scope of economic geography; classification of economic activities; Factors affecting location of Economic Activity with special reference to agriculture, manufacturing and Services; Classification of world agricultural system of Whittlesey. Vo n Thunen theory of location of agricultural activity.
Unit –II
Learning Outcome:
Evaluate the significance and relevance of theories in relation to the location of different economic activities.
Secondary Activities: Manufacturing (Cotton Textile, Iron and Steel, Petrochemical), Major Industrial Regions of the world, Special Economic Zones and Technology Parks. Tertiary Activities: Transport (Land, Air, Water and Pipelines), Trade (National and International) ; Weber and Smith’s Industrial location Theory.
Unit -III: Learning Outcome:
Define and differentiate types of resources, their management and sustainable use.
Resource ‐ Concept and Classification, Functional Theory of Resources, Distribution, Utilization, Problems and Management of World’s Resources (Land, Water, Forest and Energy (Coal, petroleum and non -conventional), Mineral resources ( Iron, Bauxite). Resource Development Regions of India; Resource depletion, conservation and Sustainable use of resources.
Unit -IV: Practical
Learning Outcome:
Create different types of thematic maps, charts and diagrams.
1. Determination and representation of agricultural efficiency (Kendall and Bhatia method).
2. Method of delineation of crop combination regions by Weaver and Doi’s method.
3. Traffic flow diagram and Drawing of Isochrones, Isotims, Isodapanes.
4. Practical Record and viva –voce.

TipClassical Models




Von Thünen’s Agricultural Model: Concentric rings of land use around a central market.

Christaller’s Central Place Theory: Hierarchical arrangement of market centers.

title: Agricultural Geography
text: Welcome to the Agricultural Geography module of Geography OpenCourseWare. This course examines the spatial organization of agriculture — including farming systems, crop distribution, land use patterns, agricultural revolutions, and contemporary challenges of food security.

title: Agricultural Geography
text:
UGC NET Syllabus

Land capability classification and Land Use Planning.
Cropping Pattern: Methods of delineating crop combination regions (Weaver, Doi and Rafiullah), Crop diversification.
Von Thunen’s Model of Land Use Planning.
Measurement and Determinants of Agricultural Productivity, Regional variations in Agricultural Productivity.
Agricultural Systems of the World.



Delimitation of agricultural regions
Content for Delimitation of agricultural regions coming soon.

title: Agricultural Geography
section: Measurement of Agricultural Productivity and Efficiency
text:
Measurement of Agricultural Productivity and Efficiency
Agricultural productivity is the measure of agricultural output per unit of input (land, labor, or capital).

Key Techniques and Scholars

1. M.G. Kendall (1939)
Proposed the Ranking Coefficient Method. He considered output per unit area (yield) across 48 administrative towns in England to determine productivity levels.


2. Sapre and Despande (1964)
Used a similar ranking order method, giving weightage to the output per unit area along with the percentage share under each crop.


3. Bhatia (1967)
Devised the Yield Index formula: \[I_y = \frac{Y_c}{Y_r} \times 100\] Where \(Y_c\) is the hectare yield of a crop in the component area and \(Y_r\) is the yield of the same crop in the entire region.


4. L.D. Stamp (1967)
Introduced the concept of the “Standard Nutrition Unit” (SNU), measuring the carrying capacity of land in terms of the population it can support, taking caloric value into account.


5. Endyedi (1964) and Shafi (1972-74)
Defined the Productivity Index to determine indices in respect of twelve food crops in India. \[Index = \frac{Y}{Y_n} \div \frac{T}{T_n}\]


6. Jasbir Singh (1972-74)
Modified previous techniques to calculate the carrying capacity of agricultural land in regions like Haryana, expressing it as a percentage of the entire region.


7. V.R. Singh (1979)
Developed a technique concerned with delimiting agricultural productivity by computing intensity and spread indices of yield, grain equivalents, and cropping patterns.

title: Agricultural Geography
text:
Crop Combinations
Crop combination is the study of the diversity of crops grown in a specific area during a specific interval of time. It provides a basis for regional planning and understanding the complexity of agricultural regions.

Major Statistical Methods

1. J.C. Weaver (1954)
Weaver’s Minimum Variance Method is the most famous statistical approach for identifying crop combinations. He compared the actual percentage of cropland under various crops with a theoretical distribution (e.g., 100% for 1 crop, 50% for 2 crops, etc.). * Formula: \(d^2 = \frac{\sum d^2}{n}\) * Used to define the crop combination of 1081 counties in his research work.


2. Weaver’s Method Modifications
Several scholars modified Weaver’s approach to overcome drawbacks like the tendency to show the lowest deviation for insignificant crops: * Doi (1959): Introduced the concept of “Standard Deviation” to substitute Weaver’s variance. * Peter Scott (1957): Suggested that animals are as important as crops when determining the combination region of Tasmania. * J.T. Coppock: Modified methods to account for all animals on the farm by converting them into equivalent units based on food consumption.


3. Other Contributors

Jasbir Singh (1971)
Singh and Dhillon (1984)
Tinath and Krishan (1966)

title: Agricultural Geography
text:
Whittlesey’s Classification of Agricultural Regions (1936)
Derwent Whittlesey proposed a comprehensive classification of the world’s agricultural regions based on five functional and structural criteria.



Whittlesey’s Classification of Agricultural Regions



Five Criteria for Classification

Crop and Livestock Combination: The specific mix of plants and animals being raised.
Methods and Techniques: The tools and practices used to grow crops and raise livestock.
Intensiveness of Inputs: The degree of application of labor, capital, and technology per unit of land.
Disposal of Products: Whether the produce is for subsistence or for sale (commercial).
Ensemble of Structures: The types of farm buildings and infrastructure used to house the operation.



Major Agricultural Regions

Nomadic Herding: Extensive subsistence livestock raising (e.g., Fulani in West Africa, Masai in East Africa, Bedouins in Saudi Arabia).
Livestock Ranching: Commercial grazing of livestock over extensive areas (e.g., Pampa in Uruguay/Argentina, Veldt in South Africa, Downs in Australia).
Shifting Cultivation: Slash-and-burn agriculture (e.g., Milpa in Central America, Ladang in Malaysia, Jhum in North-Eastern India).
Rudimentary Sedentary Tillage: Tropical subsistence farming with fixed fields.
Intensive Subsistence Agriculture: Found in monsoon-dominated regions of Asia with high population density (paddy-rice dominant).
Mediterranean Agriculture: Specialized farming in Mediterranean climates (grapes, olives, citrus).
Commercial Grain Farming: Large-scale mechanized wheat/grain production (Steppes, Prairies).
Commercial Livestock and Crop Farming: Mixed farming aimed at the market.
Commercial Dairy Farming: Specialized milk and dairy product production (e.g., in Europe and North America).
Specialized Horticulture and Olericulture: Also known as “Truck Farming” (fruits and vegetables).
Collective Farming: Jointly owned/operated holdings (e.g., Sovkhozes in the former USSR).
Commercial Plantation Farming: Large-scale export-oriented monoculture (coffee, tea, rubber, sugar).



Agricultural regionalisation
Content for Agricultural regionalisation coming soon.

title: Agricultural Geography
text:
Agricultural Revolutions
Transformative shifts in agricultural systems.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






NoteRevolutions in Farming




First Agricultural Revolution (Neolithic): The transition from hunting-gathering to settled farming ~10,000 BCE.
Second Agricultural Revolution: Mechanization, crop rotation, and enclosure movement in 18th-19th century Europe.
Green Revolution: High-Yielding Varieties (HYVs), irrigation, chemical fertilizers, and pesticides — especially in India (1960s-70s).
Gene Revolution: GMOs, biotechnology, and precision agriculture.
Evergreen Revolution: Swaminathan’s concept — productivity without ecological harm.

title: Agricultural Geography
text:
Food Security & Contemporary Issues
Challenges facing global agriculture.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






ImportantCurrent Challenges




Global Food Security: FAO definition — availability, access, utilization, and stability.
Hunger & Malnutrition: Global Hunger Index, stunting, wasting, and micronutrient deficiency.
India’s Food Security: PDS, NFSA (2013), PM-KISAN, and buffer stock management.
Climate Change & Agriculture: Shifting agro-climatic zones, crop failure risk, and adaptation strategies.
WTO & Agriculture: Agreement on Agriculture, subsidies, market access, and developing country concerns.
Organic Farming: Principles, certification, and the growth of sustainable agriculture.





Books and Further Readings
Agricultural Geography provides the spatial framework for understanding food production and livelihoods. Continue with Economic Geography and Indian Physical for related content.

title: Agricultural Geography
text:
Fundamentals of Agricultural Geography
Agricultural geography focuses on the spatial distribution of agricultural activities and the multitude of factors that influence farming choices across different regions. It acts as the bridge between human societies and their physical environments through the lens of food production.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






NoteKey Principles




Factors Affecting Agriculture: Agricultural patterns are shaped by a complex interplay of physical factors (climate, soil fertility, topography, and water availability) and human factors (labor availability, capital investment, technological access, and government policies).
Agricultural Regions (Whittlesey): Derwent Whittlesey’s classic 1936 classification identified 13 distinct agricultural regions of the world based on crop types, livestock associations, labor intensity, and the ultimate destination of the produce.
Intensive vs. Extensive Agriculture: Intensive farming involves high inputs of capital and labor relative to land area (e.g., wet rice cultivation in Asia), aiming for maximum yield per hectare. Extensive farming uses vast tracts of land with relatively low inputs per area (e.g., commercial wheat farming in the American Midwest), focusing on yield per capita.
Subsistence vs. Commercial Agriculture: Subsistence agriculture is designed primarily to provide food for direct consumption by the farmer and their family, typical in developing nations. Commercial agriculture focuses on cash-crop production for sale off the farm, dominating the developed world.




Green revolution and its socio-economic and ecological implications
Content for Green revolution and its socio-economic and ecological implications coming soon.


Infrastructure: irrigation, seeds, fertilizers, power
Content for Infrastructure: irrigation, seeds, fertilizers, power coming soon.

Rice: Monsoon Asia — India, China, Bangladesh, Indonesia, Vietnam.

title: Agricultural Geography
text:
Types of Farming
Different parts of the world have cultivated distinct farming systems adapted to their specific environmental constraints and economic demands.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






TipFarming Systems




Shifting Cultivation: Also known as slash-and-burn, this is the oldest form of agriculture (e.g., Jhum in India, Milpa in Central America). Farmers clear forest patches, burn the debris for nutrient-rich ash, and move to a new plot when soil fertility declines.
Pastoral Nomadism: A subsistence strategy based on the herding of domesticated animals over expansive dryland territories, such as the Sahel or Central Asian steppes, dictated by the seasonal availability of pasture and water.
Intensive Subsistence (Wet Rice): Dominant in densely populated Monsoon Asia. It is highly labor-intensive, relying on meticulous water management and terracing to maximize the yield of small landholdings.
Plantation Agriculture: A legacy of colonialism, involving large-scale commercial monoculture of cash crops like tea, coffee, rubber, sugarcane, and palm oil, primarily located in the tropics but owned by multinational corporations.
Commercial Grain Farming: Highly mechanized, extensive farming of single cereal crops (wheat, corn) on vast plains, such as the North American Prairies, the Pampas of Argentina, and the Steppes of Russia.
Mediterranean Agriculture: Highly specialized farming occurring along the Mediterranean Sea, California, and South Africa, focusing on high-value crops like olives, grapes, citrus fruits, and winter vegetables that thrive in mild, wet winters and hot, dry summers.




Von Thunen’s Model
Content for Von Thunen’s Model coming soon.

title: Cartography and Geo-Spatial Techniques
text:
Note



Core I Paper IV Cartography a nd Geo -Spatial Techniques
(4 Credit, Theory: 45hrs, Practical: 30hrs)
Unit -I: Learning Outcome:
Develop the ability to design and produce effective and aesthetically appealing maps using modern cartographic techniques and software. They will understand principles of cartographic design, color theory, and typography to create maps that convey spatial information clearly and accurately . Scientific basis of Cartography, needs of map making, characteristics of maps, Geographical Coordinates (Latitude and Longitude), Graticules , types of Scales (Plain, and Diagonal Scale.)
Unit -II Learning Outcome:
Acquire knowledge in selecting and applying appropriate map projections for representing geographical data accurately, as well as mastering the skills to create and interpret dip strike maps to analyze geological structures effectively.
Meaning, Uses and types of Map Projection, Transformation of area, Distance and Direction, Choice of map projection, Interpretation of Bedding plane, Strike, Dip, structure & stratigraphy of Geological map, methods of determination of slope (Wentworth’s method and Smith).
Unit -III Learning Outcome:
Gain proficiency in using GIS software to capture, store, manipulate, and analyze geospatial data. They will learn how to integrate various data layers, perform spatial queries, and create interactive maps to solve real -world geographical problems.
History of Geographical Information System, Components of GIS, Dimensions of GIS data - Conceptual (field/object) and logical (raster/vector), Data sources, data types (raster/vector/attribute), History of Remote Sensing, Types of Platform, Sensor characteri stics, Aerial photographs and Visual interpretation of Satellite images, Global Positioning System (GPS) and Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS), Applications of GIS and RS.
Unit -IV: Practical
Learning Outcome:
Acquire hands -on cum practical skills in using modern cartographic tools and geospatial technologies to create visually compelling maps and effectively analyze spatial data for various applications in geography, environmental studies, and other related fi elds. 1. Construction of Graphical, RF & Statement Scale, Diagonal Scale
2. Construction of Map Projections: Cylindrical and Conical Projection with one and two standard parallels and Zenithal.
3. Drawing of Choropleth and isopleth maps,
4. Slope Analysis (Wentworth’s method).
5. Identification and mapping of water bodies from satellite imageries
6. Digitization of map and drawing of few point, line and polygon features.
7. Practical record and viva -voce

title: Climatology
text:

Books and Further Readings
Climatology is fundamental to understanding both physical and human geography. Continue to Oceanography and Environmental for related Earth system topics.

title: Climatology
text:
Atmosphere: Structure & Composition
The Earth’s atmosphere is a complex, dynamic gaseous envelope that sustains life and drives all climatic phenomena. Understanding its vertical structure and chemical composition is fundamental to climatology.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






NoteAtmospheric Layers & Composition




Troposphere: The lowermost layer (0–12 km), where almost all weather events occur. Temperature decreases with altitude at an average environmental lapse rate of 6.5°C per kilometer. It contains 75% of the atmosphere’s mass and almost all its water vapor.
Stratosphere: Extending from the tropopause up to 50 km, this layer contains the ozone layer (ozonosphere), which absorbs harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation. Temperature inverts and increases with altitude here due to UV absorption.
Mesosphere & Thermosphere: Above the stratosphere, the mesosphere sees temperatures drop sharply, while the thermosphere experiences extreme temperature increases due to solar radiation absorption. The ionosphere within this region reflects radio waves.
Atmospheric Composition: The permanent gases include Nitrogen (78%), Oxygen (21%), and Argon (0.9%). Variable gases, though present in trace amounts, are critical for the greenhouse effect, including Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), Water Vapor, and Methane (CH₄).

title: Climatology
text:
Atmospheric Circulation
The unequal heating of the Earth’s surface creates pressure gradients, giving rise to complex global and regional wind systems that transport heat and moisture across the globe.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






ImportantWind Systems & Pressure Belts




General Circulation (Tri-Cellular Model): The Hadley Cell drives trade winds near the equator; the Ferrel Cell drives westerlies in the mid-latitudes; and the Polar Cell creates polar easterlies. This three-cell structure explains the primary latitudinal shifting of climate zones.
Pressure Belts: Include the Equatorial Low (Doldrums), Subtropical High (Horse Latitudes), Subpolar Low, and Polar High. Their seasonal migration, following the apparent movement of the sun, dictates wet and dry seasons across the tropics.
Jet Streams: Narrow, high-speed meandering air currents in the upper troposphere. The Subtropical and Polar jet streams significantly influence the paths of surface weather systems and aviation routes.
Monsoons: Massive seasonal reversals of wind direction caused by the differential heating of land and sea. The Indian Monsoon is the most prominent, fundamentally shaping the hydrology, agriculture, and economy of South Asia.
Local Winds: Thermally driven local circulations including land/sea breezes, mountain/valley anabatic and katabatic winds, and regional phenomena like the warm/dry Chinook (Rockies), Foehn (Alps), and the cold Mistral (France).




Atmospheric stability and instability.
Content for Atmospheric stability and instability. coming soon.

title: Climatology
text:
Climate Classification
Organizing the world’s climates into meaningful categories.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






TipClassification Systems




Köppen System: The most widely used system — Af, Am, Aw, BSh, BWh, Cfa, Cfb, Dfa, ET, EF.
Thornthwaite System: Based on potential evapotranspiration and moisture index.
Trewartha Modification: Refined Köppen boundaries for better representation.
Climatic Regions of India: Tropical, subtropical, arid, semi-arid, and montane zones.

title: Climatology
text:
Insolation & Heat Budget
The sun is the primary engine of Earth’s climate. The balance between incoming solar radiation and outgoing terrestrial radiation determines the average global temperature and climatic zones.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






TipSolar Energy & Earth’s Energy Balance




Insolation: Incoming solar radiation is fundamentally controlled by Earth’s spherical shape and axial tilt. Latitude determines the angle of incidence, while the season determines the daylight duration. Atmospheric transparency also affects how much radiation reaches the surface.
Albedo: This refers to the reflectivity of different Earth surfaces. Snow and ice have high albedos (reflecting up to 90% of insolation), while oceans and dark forests have low albedos, absorbing more heat. This plays a critical role in ice-albedo feedback loops.
Terrestrial Radiation: The Earth absorbs shortwave solar radiation and re-emits it as long-wave infrared radiation. Certain atmospheric gases trap this long-wave radiation, creating the necessary greenhouse effect that keeps the planet habitable.
Heat Budget: This represents the equilibrium between incoming and outgoing energy. While the whole Earth maintains a balance (ensuring it doesn’t perpetually heat or cool), the tropics receive a surplus of energy and the poles operate at a deficit, driving global wind and ocean currents to redistribute this heat.




Koppen’s Thornthwaite’s and Trewar Tha’s classification of world climate
Content for Koppen’s Thornthwaite’s and Trewar Tha’s classification of world climate coming soon.


Köppen’s scheme
Content for Köppen’s scheme coming soon.

title: Climatology
text:
Moisture & Precipitation
Water exists in all three states in the atmosphere, and its phase changes release or absorb vast amounts of latent heat, which fuels severe weather systems.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗






NoteHumidity, Clouds & Rainfall




Humidity: Measured as absolute, specific, or relative humidity. Relative humidity is crucial for human comfort and cloud formation. Condensation occurs when air cools to its dew point, leading to fog, dew, or clouds.
Cloud Formation & Types: Clouds form through convection (surface heating), orographic lift (forced over mountains), frontal wedging, or low-level convergence. High cirrus clouds indicate fair weather; layered stratus clouds bring steady drizzle; and towering cumulonimbus clouds are responsible for severe thunderstorms.
Types of Rainfall:

Convectional: Intense local showers common in equatorial regions.
Orographic: Heavy rain on the windward side of mountains, leaving a dry ‘rain shadow’ on the leeward side.
Cyclonic/Frontal: Widespread precipitation when contrasting air masses collide.

Global Patterns: High precipitation characterizes the ITCZ and monsoon domains; arid zones dominate the Subtropical Highs and deep continental interiors.

Content for Temperature and pressure belts of the world coming soon.

Content for Thornthwaite’s scheme coming soon.

text:

Understand the concept of the environmental ecosystem, their types, structure, and functions, again they can also get a clear idea about biogeochemical cycles including Carbon, Oxygen, Nitrogen cycle etc.
Environment: Concept, Types and Characteristics and Principles; Environmental Controls and Concept of tolerance; Ecosystem: Concept, Types, Structure and Functions (Food Chain and Food Web, Trophic Level, Ecological Pyramid); Energy flow in Ecosystem; Bio -geo-chemical Cycles (Nitrogen, Carbon, Oxygen); Concept and Types of Biomes: (Equatorial, Subtropical, Temperate and Polar).

Learning Outcome:

Modern period: Contributions of Alexander Von Humboldt, Carl Ritter, Ratzel, Vidal De La Blache and Mackinder.

Students will have greater understanding about different schools of thoughts enriching the subject matter of geography.
Dichotomy and dualism in Geography – Environmental Determinism and Possibilism, Systematic and Regional Geography, Ideographic and Nomothetic, Physical and Human Geography. Recent Trends in Geography – Quantitative Revolution, Behaviouralism and Radicalism.
Unit -IV: Practical
Learning Outcome:
Students will have greater understanding through report writing and seminar presentation on various geographical perspectives, approaches and methods from ancient classical period to the present scenario.
Submission of Project Report and Presentation on any of the following:
1. Analysis and Comparison of areas of geographical works between Classical Greek vs Roman Geographers.
2. Analysis of different perspectives in historical development of geographical thinking in the modern period.
3. Analysis of different perspectives, approaches and methods involved in the advancement of geographical thinking in recent times.
4. Report and Viva -Voce.

UGC NET Syllabus
